data.title: Argentina's YPF says 2024 adjusted EBIDTA up 15% to $4.65 billion
data.description:
Argentina's state-oil firm YPF reported on Friday a 15% rise in adjusted full-year earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortisation (EBITDA) to $4.65 billion.

It attributed the rise to the recovery of local fuel prices, growing oil exports and the expansion of its shale oil production.

The company said oil production from Vaca Muerta, one of the largest shale oil and gas formations in the world, rose 26% to an average of 122,000 barrels per day in 2024, reaching 138,000 bpd in the last months of the year.

It added that its exports to Chile reached an average of 35,000 bpd, a jump of 174% compared to the previous year.

The company said its 2024 investment figure of $5.04 billion was on target and 63.5% of that went to non-conventional assets in Vaca Muerta.

YPF said that it issued $800 million in bonds in January 2024 as well as $540 million in September. It issued another $1.1 billion in bonds this January to refinance $757 million in debt and acquire 54% of Sierra Chata, one of the most promising gas blocks in Vaca Muerta.

data.title: Asure Software Price Target Maintained With a $20.00/Share by Needham
Forward prices see uptick on lower wind, cold weather forecast
data.description:
Nordic forward prices were on track to post weekly losses even as they rose on Friday amid forecasts of colder weather and lower wind output over the coming weeks.

* The Nordic front-quarter baseload power contract (ENOFBLQc1) rose 0.55 euros to 19.95 euros per megawatt hour (MWh) as of 1047 GMT, but was down more than 5% for the week.

* Nordic front-year prices (ENOFBLYc1) gained 0.15 euros to 35.9 euros per MWh, but were down 2% for the week, headed for their third straight week of losses.

* The reason for this rise in the Nordic markets "is quite a bit more bullish weather (with) high pressure both in Germany and the Nordics next week with low wind power output and colder weather," said Ole Tom Djupskaas, a power analyst at LSEG.

* Nordic water reserves available 15 days ahead (PCAEC00) were seen at 14.34 terawatt hours (TWh) above normal, compared with 15.52 TWh on Thursday.

* "The week after next will see milder and only partially unsettled weather in the second half of the month," Georg Muller, a meteorologist at LSEG, said in a note adding that "April will likely be also milder than normal and only weakly active."

* European wholesale gas prices rebounded amid strikes on gas infrastructure in Ukraine after falling to their lowest level in five months in early trade.

* In the European carbon market, the benchmark contract (CFI2Zc1) rose 1.38 euros to 68.7 euros a tonne.

* German year-ahead power (TRDEBYc1) rose 2.55 euros to 82.5 euros per MWh.

* The Nordic power price for next-day physical delivery (FXSYSAL=NPX), or system price, rose 15.2 euros to 34.76 euros per MWh.

data.title: China's Evergrande Property Services expects up to 37% decline in annual profit
data.description:
China's Evergrande Property Services Group said on Friday that it expects to record a decrease in its annual profit for the year ended December 31, 2024, as it incurred legal expenses in relation to the 13.4 billion yuan ($1.85 billion) deposit pledge, among others.

The property developer expects to record an unaudited net profit between 980 million yuan and 1,120 million yuan in fiscal 2024 compared 1,563.8 million yuan from a year before.

($1 = 7.2350 Chinese yuan renminbi)

data.title: Tianju Dihe (Suzhou) Technology Expects Up to 51% Higher Net Profit in 2024
data.description:
Tianju Dihe (Suzhou) Technology expects a net profit between 50 million yuan and 53 million yuan for 2024, 43% to 51% higher than 35 million yuan from 2023, a Friday bourse filing said.

The tech company attributed the expected increase to a growth in its revenue from the application programming interface marketplace and data management solutions sector.

data.title: Q-linea Secures Testing System Contract with US Customer
data.description:
Q-linea signed a contract with a US-based cancer and pediatric hospital facility for its rapid antimicrobial susceptibility testing system, ASTar.

System installation is scheduled for late March, training and evaluation activities will start in April, and clinical implementation is expected within 2025, the Swedish medical diagnostics company said Friday.

data.title: Marathon Petroleum Director Evan Bayh Buys 1,000 Shares
data.description:
Reporter NameBayh Evan
RelationshipDirector
TypePurchase
Amount$133,700
SEC FilingForm 4

Marathon Petroleum Corp Director, Evan Bayh, purchased 1,000 shares of common stock on March 5, 2025, at a price of $133.7 per share, totaling $133,700. Following this transaction, Bayh directly owns 69,304 shares of Marathon Petroleum Corp.

SEC Filing: Marathon Petroleum Corp [ MPC ] - Form 4 - Mar. 07, 2025

data.title: Euro poised for best week since 2009 as dollar weakens ahead of jobs data
data.description:
Key points:
  • Dollar index at four-month low
  • Safe havens yen and Swiss Franc perk up

The euro was set for its best week in 16 years against the dollar on Friday, boosted by Germany's game-changing fiscal reforms, while worries over growth and tariffs drove the greenback to a four-month low ahead of U.S. jobs data.

It has been a volatile week for the currency market, driven mainly by U.S. trade and economic growth uncertainties and a pivotal development in Europe as its largest economy abandoned its fiscal constraints to boost spending and revive growth.

"This week is a watershed moment ...because we've lived through a couple of years of dollar and U.S. growth exceptionalism," said Kenneth Broux, head of corporate research FX and rates at Societe Generale.

Following a slew of mixed economic data out of the United States so far this week, the focus on Friday falls on U.S. nonfarm payrolls numbers as market participants will assess the health of the economy and its implications on inflation and interest rates.

The U.S. dollar index has fallen more than 3.5% this week to its lowest since early November.

The euro, on the other hand, has risen more than 4.5% and was set for its biggest weekly jump since March 2009. It was perched at its highest since early November and was last up 0.6% on the day at $1.08520.

"Right now we're in a situation where investors are buying dips in euro/dollar and I think payrolls today can only accelerate the move higher," Broux said.

"I do not think that a stronger NFP print is going to stop this move higher in euro/dollar. It could slow it, but I don't think it's going to change the trend."

The European Central Bank's hawkish rate cut and surging European bond yields on the back of Germany's massive spending proposal has helped lift the common currency.

BofA Global Research raised its year-end forecast for the euro to $1.15, from $1.10 previously.

The pound headed for its worst weekly performance against the euro since 2023, while it strengthened against the dollar.

'FALLEN OUT OF FAVOUR'

Another reprieve of levies aimed at Mexico and Canada announced by U.S. President Donald Trump on Thursday offered little relief to whiplashed markets.

The greenback edged 0.1% higher on the Canadian dollar to C$1.4317 but slipped 0.2% against the Mexican peso to 20.2276 pesos.

The exemption expires on April 2 when Trump said he will impose reciprocal tariffs on all U.S. trading partners.

The dollar has "fallen out of favour" amid the uncertainty, with the perceived inflationary impact of tariffs no longer enough to support it, said Kieran Williams, head of Asia FX at InTouch Capital Markets.

"Ahead of the NFP survey, evidence has tilted towards a softer outcome. If this transpires it could spook markets further," he said.

Against a backdrop of federal job culls, the U.S. likely added 160,000 jobs in February compared with 143,000 in January, while the unemployment rate is expected to have held steady at 4.0%, economists forecast in a Reuters poll.

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell will be able to follow up the jobs report when he speaks later in the day on the economic outlook.

Markets currently have three Fed rate cuts priced in for the rest of the year.

The safe-haven yen is at its strongest against the greenback since early October, while the Swiss franc hit a three-month peak of 0.8838 .

Japan's economy minister Ryosei Akazawa said the nation has cleared the key threshold for the government to officially declare an end to long-term price deflation.

Inflationary pressure from wage gains and prolonged rises in food costs could prompt Bank of Japan board members to discuss another interest rate hike as soon as in May, three sources familiar with its thinking told Reuters.

Elsewhere in Asia, the offshore yuan steadied at 7.2346. China's exports slowed over the January-February period and imports unexpectedly contracted, official data on Friday shows, as trade tensions escalated with the United States.

The risk-sensitive Australian dollar slid 0.2% to $0.6320.

data.title: Morgan Stanley Trims Price Target on Commercial Metals to $53 From $56, Keeps Equalweight Rating
data.description: Commercial Metals has an average rating of overweight and mean price target of $59.13, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

data.title: inTEST Board Renews $10 Million Stock Buyback Program
data.description:
inTEST said Friday its board renewed the company's $10 million share repurchase plan, which has roughly $9 million remaining.

The board initially commenced the buyback program in November 2023, the company added.

data.title: Foghorn Therapeutics Price Target Maintained With a $13.00/Share by HC Wainwright & Co.
MDA Space Posts Q4 Adjusted Profit, Revenue Beat
data.description:
MDA Space on Friday reported higher adjusted profit and revenue for its fourth-quarter that beat analysts' estimates.

Adjusted net income of $35.1 million or $0.28 per share, was 26% higher than the $27.8 million, or $0.23 per share. The result beat the consensus estimate of $0.26 per share, according to FactSet.

Revenue rose 70% to $346.6 million, driven by strong contributions from Satellite Systems business, and beating the consensus analyst forecast of $319.9 million.

MDA's backlog as of the end of the quarter was $4.39 billion, up from $3.1 billion.

The company expects full-year 2025 revenue to be between $1.50 billion to $1.65 billion, up from $1.08 billion in 2024.

MDA expects first-quarter revenue to be $315 million to $335 million as it executes on its backlog.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Friday at 7 AM ET
data.description:
U.S Futures Inch Upward Amid Trade Policy Uncertainty

U.S. stock futures nudged up after seesawing trade policy hit stocks on Wall Street Thursday, with strong after-hours earnings from Broadcom a potential bright spot as investors increasingly fret about growth.

Powell Set to Speak After Job Numbers and Tariff Turmoil

It is the Fed chair's last opportunity to talk about the shifting economic landscape before the March policymaking meeting.

Eurozone Grew More Strongly Than Thought at End of 2024

The stronger-than-expected reading was thanks to signs of more robust growth in some members of the currency area, including Ireland and Italy.

German Factory Orders Slump Again as Hopes Grow for Stimulus to Counter Tariff Threat

The drop points to a continued slump in a sector whose hope for a rebound lies in lower interest rates and a bump in government spending.

Big Chunk of North American Trade Remains Exposed to Tariffs

The Trump reprieve applies to products covered under a 2020 pact, which leaves a lot of goods still subject to the new 25% tariff levels.

It's a Surprisingly Good Time to Be in the Property Insurance Business

Insurers, despite inflation and recent disasters, are reporting strong results thanks to pricing and coverage adjustments.

Bank of Russia's Inflation Struggle Has Lessons for ECB

Will the European Central Bank face a problem that has bedeviled the Bank of Russia?

Washington Signals Plans to Open Private Funds to More Investors

Momentum is building in the Trump administration and Congress to change rules blocking most Americans from investing in private equity.

China's Export Growth Disappoints Amid Trade Tensions

China's export growth over the first two months of the year was weaker than expected, as shipments were curtailed by rising trade tensions with the U.S.

Trump Pares Back Canada, Mexico Tariffs in Latest Whipsaw on Trade

Goods covered by the countries' USMCA agreement get a reprieve, but levies remain in effect for many goods, including oil.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Financial Services News of the Day - Friday at 7 AM ET
data.description:
U.K. Pension Manager NEST Plots Private-Markets Investing Spree

The fast-growing defined-contribution plan aims to pump billions into alternative assets while avoiding heavy fees.

Blackstone Raises Largest Commercial Property Debt Fund With $8 Billion Haul

The fund took two years to build and will be active in North America, Europe and Australia.

Private Equity Wants a Piece of Your 401(k)

Companies like Apollo and KKR are angling for retirement savers with new funds. Investors in 401(k)s should be wary.

Blackstone vs. BlackRock: The Greatest Wall Street Frenemy Story Ever Told

Stephen Schwarzman and Larry Fink are increasingly all up in each other's business. Just look at BlackRock's new Panama Canal deal.

Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on Malaysian banks and more in the latest Market Talks covering financial services.

Trump Signs Executive Order Officially Establishing Crypto Reserve

The U.S.'s stockpile will favor bitcoin and won't include purchases that cost taxpayers money, the Trump administration says.

The Polarizing Crypto Executive Who Wooed His Way Into Trump's Orbit

Ripple CEO Brad Garlinghouse stands to benefit from the inclusion of the XRP token in a U.S. digital-asset stockpile.

How the Biggest Crypto Hack Ever Nearly Destroyed the World's No. 2 Exchange

Bybit survived, thanks to emergency loans from its peers, but questions over the crypto industry's security linger.

Australia's Insignia Opens Books to Bain After $2.1 Billion Proposal

Bain Capital will get a look at Insignia Financial's books after it sweetened its offer for the Australian wealth manager to about US$2.12 billion.

Interim SEC Chair Uyeda Sees Lighter Role for the Regulator

Mark Uyeda declined to discuss the effect of staff cuts on SEC enforcement.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Energy News of the Day - Friday at 7 AM ET
data.description:
Oil Set for Weekly Loss Amid U.S. Tariffs, OPEC+ Output Plans

Oil prices rose, but remained on track for a significant weekly loss amid fluctuating U.S. trade policies and OPEC+ plans to hike output.

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on oil futures and more in the latest Market Talks covering energy and utilities

Harbour Energy Shares Sink to Record Low After Profit Hit by U.K. Windfall Tax

The oil-and-gas producer swung to a loss in 2024 as the energy profits levy continued to drag on results and its CEO described the U.K. as a difficult fiscal and regulatory environment in which to operate.

Sunnova Bondholders Engage Advisers Following KKR Deal, Going-Concern Warning

A group of bondholders in Sunnova Energy International has organized and hired legal counsel after the rooftop solar panel company issued a going concern notice and said it raised financing from KKR.

U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Rise More Than Expected

U.S. crude oil inventories rose by 3.6 million barrels last week, which was more than expected, resuming an upward trend after a surprise drawdown the week before.

DTEK in Talks With U.S. Suppliers to Boost Gas Supply to Europe

Ukraine's largest private energy company is in discussions with U.S. suppliers to bring more natural gas to Europe as part of a broader expansion, its CEO said in an interview.

Nuclear Power's Revival Is Here. What Do You Do With All the Radioactive Waste?

Tons of radioactive refuse go into temporary storage every year, and new efforts to store nuclear waste away from reactor sites are getting pushback.

Oil Outlook Clouded by OPEC+ Output, Trade Tariff Risks

Higher-than-expected supply and a potential demand hit due to softer U.S. activity and escalating tariffs pose downside risks to its oil price forecast, said Goldman Sachs.

U.S. Refiners Import Lots of Oil. Trump's Tariffs Spell Trouble.

Supplies from Canada and Mexico are at the heart of the U.S. oil industry.

Adnoc, OMV Reach Deal to Form $60 Billion Petrochemical Giant

Abu Dhabi's Adnoc and Austria's OMV reached an agreement to combine their petrochemical businesses and subsequently acquire Canada's Nova Chemicals to form a giant with a combined value of about $60 billion.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Friday at 7 AM ET
data.description:
Blackstone Raises Largest Commercial Property Debt Fund With $8 Billion Haul

The fund took two years to build and will be active in North America, Europe and Australia.

Broadcom Earnings and AI Strength Should Win Over a Tougher Crowd

Revenue from the company's custom AI chips is soaring, with more potential customers in the pipeline.

Walgreens Seals $10 Billion Take-Private Deal With Sycamore

One of the biggest leveraged buyouts in the past decade would try to revive the pharmacy icon in private hands.

British American Tobacco's Canada Litigation Plan Gets Court Approval

A Canadian court approved the company's plan to settle outstanding litigation in the country.

Universal Music Shares Rise on Subscription Revenue Beat

Stock in the record label behind Taylor Swift rose after revenue from subscriptions and streaming grew 5.6%.

Salvatore Ferragamo's Shares Plunge

Shares in the luxury fashion firm tumbled as the company continues to struggle with a brand revamp strategy.

Tesla's Fortunes Fall as Musk Rises in Trump World

The CEO's politics have eroded the brand's appeal among some buyers of electric vehicles, surveys indicate. "I used to idolize the guy," said one Tesla driver who wants to get out of his lease.

Australia's Insignia Opens Books to Bain After $2.1 Billion Proposal

Bain Capital will get a look at Insignia Financial's books after it sweetened its offer for the Australian wealth manager to about US$2.12 billion.

SpaceX Again Loses Spacecraft During Latest Starship Test Flight

The explosion disrupted flights in the Caribbean. Elon Musk's company dealt with a similar setback during a January flight.

HPE to Eliminate 2,500 Jobs as Tariffs Hurt Fiscal Outlook

Chief Financial Officer Marie Myers said the outlook reflects the latest tariffs on goods from China, Canada and Mexico.

data.title: Fed Chair Powell Set to Speak After Jobs Data; ECB Cuts Rates to Guard Economy Against Tariff Threats
data.description:
Fed Chair Powell Set to Speak After Jobs Data; ECB Cuts Rates to Guard Economy Against Tariff Threats By Vicky Ge Huang

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell will address the 18th annual U.S. Monetary Policy Forum on Friday in New York City, just hours after the Bureau of Labor Statistics provides the latest update on the health of the labor market.

The February jobs report is due at 8:30 a.m. ET. Nonfarm payrolls likely grew by 170,000 jobs, according to consensus estimates, up from 143,000 in January. Unemployment is seen steady at 4%.

Fed governor Christopher Waller, speaking at The Wall Street Journal's CFO Network Summit on Thursday, said that despite inflation's bumpy course down toward the Fed's 2% target, slowing price increases should allow the central bank to resume bringing rates lower.

Meanwhile, President Trump signed an executive order Thursday officially establishing a strategic bitcoin reserve and a stockpile for other cryptocurrencies, following through on a campaign promise to an industry that helped him get elected.

And in Europe, the European Central Bank cut interest rates to boost growth, with the region's stalled economy facing twin shocks from Trump tariff threats and a sudden need to radically increase military spending.

Top News Powell Set to Speak After Job Numbers and Tariff Turmoil

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell is scheduled to begin his keynote address at 12:30 p.m. ET and then take questions from a moderator following his prepared remarks.

It is his final opportunity to provide some public context on how policymakers are weighing the shifting economic landscape, including significant trade policy updates, persistent inflation, and waning consumer sentiment. The Federal Open Market Committee is set to meet on March 18-19, and the central bank's blackout period starts on Saturday.

Traders currently see an 89% chance that the central bank keeps the fed funds rate steady at the conclusion of the March policy meeting, according to CME's FedWatch tool.

Organized by the University of Chicago Booth School of Business, the annual conference aims to bring together policymakers, leading economic scholars, and market analysts to discuss U.S. monetary policy. The focus of this year's meeting is a new report titled "Monetary Policy Transmission to Real Activity" produced by a panel of scholars.

Before Powell's keynote presentation, Governor Michelle Bowman and New York Fed President John Williams will join discussions on the latest research. Former Cleveland Fed President Loretta Mester will lead a panel discussion on central bank framework reviews in the afternoon. (Barron's)

Fed's Chris Waller Says 'Good-News Rate Cuts' Are Still Expected

Investor sentiment has turned gloomier this week, but even if no economic downturn materializes, progress on inflation should allow the Federal Reserve to continue cutting interest rates , Fed governor Christopher Waller said at The Wall Street Journal's CFO Network Summit on Thursday.

ECB Cuts Rates to Guard Stalled Economy Against Tariff Threats

The ECB reduced its key interest rate to 2.5% from 2.75% Thursday, widening a gap in benchmark borrowing costs with the Federal Reserve. It was the sixth cut in seven meetings.

ECB President Christine Lagarde kept open the door to a pause on interest rates at the bank's next policy meeting in April, or at subsequent meetings. "We are now moving to a more evolutionary approach [where] we take account of the journey which we have traveled," she said at a news conference in Frankfurt.

Bank of Russia's Inflation Struggle Has Lessons for ECB By Paul Hannon

Will the European Central Bank face a problem that has bedeviled the Bank of Russia?

European governments are about to embark on a big increase in defense spending, fueled by growing doubts about the reliability of the U.S. as a military ally, and associated worries about the opening that absence of support might present to Vladimir Putin's Russia.

The increase in spending is likely to divert resources from other purposes, including scarce workers. In the process, it is likely to raise the eurozone economy's growth rate, and push consumer prices higher. Read more .

U.S. Economy Trump Pares Back Canada, Mexico Tariffs in Latest Whipsaw on Trade

The U.S. partially pulled back tariffs on some goods from Mexico and Canada after markets sank and companies lobbied President Trump, as the administration's swerving trade policy strained relations with allies and raised recession fears.

Big Chunk of North American Trade Remains Exposed to Tariffs U.S. Trade Deficit Grew 34% in January

The U.S. trade deficit widened in January , as import growth dwarfed a smaller increase in exports. The deficit came in at $131.4 billion, 34% greater than the $98.1 billion deficit in December.

Key Development Around the World China Scolds 'Two-Faced' U.S. in Rebuke of Trump's Agenda

China's top diplomat chided the Trump administration and said efforts to contain the country's rise were destined to fail , presenting Beijing as a force for global stability in a rejection of U.S. policy under President Trump.

China Is Secretly Worried Trump Will Win on Trade China's Export Growth Disappoints Amid Trade Tensions Canada Pulls Back Partly on U.S. Tariff Retaliation

Canadian Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc says his country would delay the implementation of retaliatory tariffs targeting about $87 billion of U.S. imports that were set to take effect in late March. "We continue to work for the removal of all tariffs," he said, via a post on X.

Panama Port Deal Plants U.S. Flag in China-Dominated Sector

A deal to put U.S. investors in control of a global ports network, including berths at the Panama Canal, removes potential levers for Chinese influence and marks rare American inroads in a strategic sector dominated by Beijing.

RBA Cautiously Weighing Unpredictable Global Storms By James Glynn

The governor of the Reserve Bank of Australia, Michele Bullock, would have navigated her way to work this week through rain falling across Sydney courtesy of a cyclone menacing Brisbane to the north.

Cyclones are a feature of Australia's summer months, but rarely do they venture so far south that they directly impact densely populated areas such as the Queensland state capital.

While Cyclone Alfred is a significant threat, Bullock will also be gazing out of her office window to storms now brewing rapidly in the global economy. Read more .

Financial Regulation Trump Signs Executive Order Officially Establishing Crypto Reserve

Thursday's order makes the U.S. one of the few nations to have a crypto reserve , in part because of how difficult stockpiles can be to manage and secure, and in part because of skepticism of the value of cryptocurrencies among many government officials worldwide.

The Polarizing Crypto Exec Who Wooed His Way Into Trump's Orbit New GOP Bill Aims to End 'Debanking' of Crypto Firms, Conservatives How the Biggest Crypto Hack Ever Nearly Destroyed the World's No. 2 Exchange Interim SEC Chair Uyeda Sees Lighter Role for the Regulator

At a Thursday financial forum, the interim chair of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission Mark Uyeda cheerfully discussed the less-meddling role he sees ahead for the securities regulator. When asked about the impact of the Trump administration's staff cuts on SEC enforcement, however, Uyeda raced to the elevator, calling over his shoulder, "No comment." Like other federal agencies, the SEC is downsizing. A market regulator like the SEC runs on people. It remains to be seen whether deep cuts will affect its investor protection work. (Barron's)

Forward Guidance Friday (all times ET)

8:30 a.m.: U.S. Employment Report

10:15 a.m.: Fed's Bowman speaks at monetary policy forum in New York

12:20 p.m.: Fed's Kugler speaks at Bank of Portugal conference in Lisbon

12:30 p.m.: Fed's Powell speaks at monetary policy forum in New York

3 p.m.: Consumer Credit

6 p.m.: President Trump delivers remarks at White House Crypto Summit

Monday

10 a.m.: Employment Trends Index

7 p.m.: Federal Reserve Board adopts new Fedwire Funds Service message format

Commentary Trump's Golden Age Begins With a Brutal Trade War

President Trump won last fall's election on the pledge of a new "golden age." Public confidence perked up and the stock markets leapt.

This week showed the dark side of that promised golden age, writes Greg Ip for The Wall Street Journal. On Tuesday, as Trump boasted to Congress that "America's momentum is back," he was allowing steep new tariffs on Mexico and Canada to take effect, initiating what may become the most brutal trade war since the 1930s.

Stocks have largely surrendered their postelection euphoria, consumer confidence has wilted, and economists talk of stagflation-a spell of slow to stagnant growth and higher inflation.

Mindful of the fallout, Trump's advisers have pressed for ways to delay or modify the tariffs. A 30-day carve-out for autos was announced Wednesday, and on Thursday Trump said tariffs on some Mexican and Canadian goods would be delayed until April 2.

Don't assume, though, that anything will fundamentally change. Trump is early in his term, enjoys complete control of his party and Congress, and is counting on tax cuts to revive confidence. Lower interest rates and oil prices may soften the sting of tariffs. All that gives him freedom to indulge his most deeply held instincts on trade. Read more .

Research Credit Spreads Expected to Widen Amid Geopolitical Uncertainty Credit spreads, or risk premiums on corporate bonds, are expected to widen as economic and geopolitical uncertainty grows around the globe, Chris Iggo, CIO at AXA Investment Managers, says in a note. The economic outlook and inflation expectations are increasingly uncertain following U.S. tariffs on key trade partners, he says. U.S. credit spreads are likely to widen to a greater extent due to extremely tight levels relative to other credit assets, he says. "The U.S. market is most at risk as these valuation metrics have been more extreme there than in other markets," Iggo says. - Miriam Mukuru

Basis Points Many of the 30,000 migrants in southern Mexico are trying to find work in the country-at least until American policy changes- but they face limited choices . The eurozone economy grew a little more than previously estimated at the end of last year thanks to signs of more robust growth in some members of the currency area. Orders at German factories fell back sharply at the start of the year , pointing to a continued slump in a beleaguered sector whose hope for a rebound lies in lower interest rates and a bump in government spending. U.K. retail footfall slightly improved in February , helped by an increase in retail parks visits, according to a report published Friday. China's foreign-exchange reserves edged up in February, in part thanks to a stronger yuan last month. The country's foreign-exchange reserves rose $18.2 billion to $3.227 trillion at the end of February, the People's Bank of China said Friday. (Dow Jones Newswires) Executive Insights

Here is our weekly roundup of stories from across WSJ Pro that we think you'll find useful.

There's a new buzzword in sustainability circles when it comes to investing in renewables and clean technologies: resilience.

AI, of all things, may help video calls feel more human .

Big tech platforms are moving toward more automation in ad sales , despite a litany of complaints from marketers.

"Animal spirits" look set to drive a revival in private-equity dealmaking, but the pickup in activity many expect may not be felt equally across the industry. See more in WSJ Pro Private Equity's Special Report on the Year Ahead .

About Us

WSJ Pro Central Banking brings you central banking news, analysis and insights from WSJ's global team of reporters and editors. This newsletter was compiled by markets reporter Vicky Ge Huang in New York. Send your tips, suggestions and feedback to [vicky.huang@wsj.com].

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today.

data.title: Ellington Financial Declares Common and Preferred Dividends
data.description:
Ellington Financial Declares Common and Preferred Dividends

OLD GREENWICH, Conn.--(BUSINESS WIRE)--March 07, 2025--

Ellington Financial Inc. ("we") today announced that its Board of Directors has declared the following: (i) a monthly dividend of $0.13 per share of common stock, payable on April 25, 2025 to common stockholders of record as of March 31, 2025; (ii) a quarterly dividend of $0.609031 per share on the Company's Series A Floating Rate Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Stock, payable on April 30, 2025 to Series A preferred stockholders of record as of March 31, 2025; (iii) a quarterly dividend of $0.390625 per share on the Company's 6.250% Series B Fixed-Rate Reset Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Stock, payable on April 30, 2025 to Series B preferred stockholders of record as of March 31, 2025; (iv) a quarterly dividend of $0.5390625 per share on the Company's 8.625% Series C Fixed-Rate Reset Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Stock, payable on April 30, 2025 to Series C preferred stockholders of record as of March 31, 2025; and (v) a quarterly dividend of $0.4375 per share on the Company's 7.00% Series D Cumulative Perpetual Redeemable Preferred Stock, payable on March 30, 2025 to Series D preferred stockholders of record as of March 20, 2025.

Cautionary Statement Regarding Forward-Looking Statements

This press release contains forward-looking statements within the meaning of the safe harbor provisions of the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995. Forward-looking statements involve numerous risks and uncertainties. Our actual results may differ from its beliefs, expectations, estimates, and projections and, consequently, you should not rely on these forward-looking statements as predictions of future events. Forward-looking statements are based on our beliefs, assumptions and expectations of our future operations, business strategies, performance, financial condition, liquidity and prospects, taking into account information currently available to us. These beliefs, assumptions, and expectations are subject to risks and uncertainties and can change as a result of many possible events or factors, not all of which are known to us. If a change occurs, our business, financial condition, liquidity, results of operations and strategies may vary materially from those expressed or implied in our forward-looking statements. The following factors are examples of those that could cause actual results to vary from our forward-looking statements: changes in interest rates and the market value of our investments, market volatility, changes in mortgage default rates and prepayment rates, our ability to borrow to finance our assets, changes in government regulations affecting our business, our ability to maintain our exclusion from registration under the Investment Company Act of 1940, our ability to maintain our qualification as a real estate investment trust, or "REIT," and other changes in market conditions and economic trends, such as changes to fiscal or monetary policy, heightened inflation, slower growth or recession, and currency fluctuations. Furthermore, forward-looking statements are subject to risks and uncertainties, including, among other things, those described under Item 1A of our Annual Report on Form 10-K, which can be accessed through our website at www.ellingtonfinancial.com or at the SEC's website (www.sec.gov). This release and the information contained herein do not constitute an offer of any securities or solicitation of an offer to purchase securities.

About Ellington Financial

Ellington Financial invests in a diverse array of financial assets, including residential and commercial mortgage loans and mortgage-backed securities, reverse mortgage loans, mortgage servicing rights and related investments, consumer loans, asset-backed securities, collateralized loan obligations, non-mortgage and mortgage-related derivatives, debt and equity investments in loan origination companies, and other strategic investments. Ellington Financial is externally managed and advised by Ellington Financial Management LLC, an affiliate of Ellington Management Group, L.L.C.

data.title: Ellington Credit Declares Monthly Common Dividend
data.description:
Ellington Credit Declares Monthly Common Dividend

OLD GREENWICH, Conn.--(BUSINESS WIRE)--March 07, 2025--

Ellington Credit Company ("we") today announced that its Board of Trustees has declared a monthly common dividend of $0.08 per share, payable on April 25, 2025 to shareholders of record as of March 31, 2025.

Cautionary Statement Regarding Forward-Looking Statements

This press release contains forward-looking statements within the meaning of the safe harbor provisions of the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995. Forward-looking statements are not historical in nature and can be identified by words such as "anticipate, " "estimate," "will," "should," "may," "expect," "project," "believe," "intend," "seek," "plan" and similar expressions or their negative forms, or by references to strategy, plans, or intentions. Forward-looking statements are based on our beliefs, assumptions and expectations of our future operations, business strategies, performance, financial condition, liquidity and prospects, taking into account information currently available to us. These beliefs, assumptions, and expectations are subject to numerous risks and uncertainties and can change as a result of many possible events or factors, not all of which are known to us. If a change occurs, our business, financial condition, liquidity, results of operations and strategies may vary materially from those expressed or implied in our forward-looking statements. The following factors are examples of those that could cause actual results to vary from those stated or implied by our forward-looking statements: changes in interest rates and the market value of our investments, market volatility, changes in the default rates on corporate loans, our ability to borrow to finance our assets, changes in government regulations affecting our business, our ability to maintain our exclusion from registration under the Investment Company Act of 1940, our ability to pivot our investment strategy to focus on collateralized loan obligations ("CLOs"), a deterioration in the CLO market, our ability to utilize our net operating loss carryforwards, our ability to convert to a closed end fund/RIC, and other changes in market conditions and economic trends, such as changes to fiscal or monetary policy, heightened inflation, slower growth or recession, and currency fluctuations. Furthermore, as stated above, forward-looking statements are subject to numerous risks and uncertainties, including, among other things, those described under Item 1A of our Annual Report on Form 10-K, which can be accessed through the link to our SEC filings under "For Investors" on our website (at www.ellingtoncredit.com) or at the SEC's website (www.sec.gov). Other risks, uncertainties, and factors that could cause actual results to differ materially from those projected or implied may be described from time to time in reports we file with the SEC, including reports on Forms 10-Q, 10-K and 8-K. We undertake no obligation to update or revise any forward-looking statements, whether as a result of new information, future events, or otherwise.

This release and the information contained herein do not constitute an offer of any securities or solicitation of an offer to purchase securities.

About Ellington Credit Company

Ellington Credit Company, formerly known as Ellington Residential Mortgage REIT, was initially formed as a real estate investment trust ("REIT") that invested primarily in residential mortgage-backed securities ("MBS"). On March 29, 2024, the Company's Board of Trustees approved a strategic transformation of its investment strategy to focus on corporate CLOs, with an emphasis on mezzanine debt and equity tranches. In connection with this transformation, the Company revoked its election to be taxed as a REIT effective January 1, 2024, and rebranded to Ellington Credit Company. At a special meeting on January 17, 2025, shareholders approved the Company's conversion to a Delaware registered closed-end fund to be treated as a regulated investment company under the Internal Revenue Code. The Company expects the conversion to be completed on or before April 1, 2025.

Ellington Credit Company is externally managed and advised by Ellington Credit Company Management LLC, an affiliate of Ellington Management Group, L.L.C.

data.title: Biocon Unit Partners with Civica to Expand Insulin Aspart Access in the US
data.description:
Biocon Biologics, a global biosimilars company and subsidiary of Biocon (, BOM: 532523), has partnered with not-for-profit pharmaceutical company Civica to enhance the accessibility and affordability of Insulin Aspart in the United States.

Under the agreement, Biocon Biologics will supply Insulin Aspart drug substance to Civica, which will then produce the drug product at its Petersburg, Virginia facility, according to a Thursday release filed with the Indian bourses.

Civica will commercialize the rapid-acting insulin analog following the completion of development work and clinical trials. No technology transfer is involved in the collaboration, the release said.

data.title: Market Chatter: Blackstone Closes $8 Billion Commercial Real-Estate Debt Fund
data.description:
Blackstone closed on an $8 billion commercial real-estate debt fund this week, The Wall Street Journal reported Friday.

The investment vehicle will be active in North America, Europe and Australia, according to the report. It makes property loans and purchases current loans.

Blackstone did not immediately respond to MT Newswires' request for comment.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: RBC Raises Price Target on South Bow to CA$38 From CA$34, Keeps Outperform Rating
data.description: South Bow's Canadian stock has an average rating of hold and mean price target of CA$36.26, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

data.title: S&P Revises Outlook on Posco, Subsidiaries to Negative on Industry Risks, Higher Leverage
data.description:
S&P Global Ratings revised its outlook on Posco Holdings and subsidiaries Posco and Posco International to negative from stable, while affirming their respective long-term issuer credit ratings at A- and BBB+.

The negative outlook reflects the potential credit impact of oversupply problems in the steel industry as well as Posco Holdings' weak electric vehicle (EV) battery material segment and increased debt burden, S&P said in a Thursday release.

Uncertainties regarding US steel tariffs and end-market demand offset potential improvements in the company's profitability, the rating agency said.

Posco Holdings' substantial capital investments, particularly in EV battery materials, will limit the improvement in its leverage, keeping adjusted debt-to-EBITDA ratio above 1.5x over the next one to two years.

However, asset sales and possible anti-dumping moves in Korea could balance some of the risks, S&P said.

Notable shifts in the company's profitability and cash flow levels may trigger future rating actions, according to S&P.

data.title: Jobs Report, Powell Speech and What Else to Watch — WSJ
data.description:
By Chelsey Dulaney

Don't bet on a quiet end to this tumultuous week for global markets.

The widely eyed monthly jobs report is due this morning, and is expected to show hiring picked up in February, with around 170,000 jobs being added. Investors are braced for a big stock swing after the release, options-market data suggests.

The report comes as investors fret over the U.S. economic outlook. The Trump administration's swerving trade policy is weighing on growth expectations and consumer and business confidence, survey data suggests. The latest U-turn came Thursday, when the White House granted a tariff reprieve on some imports from Mexico and Canada until April 2.

Stock futures pointed to a higher open on Wall Street early Friday. But indexes are on track for weekly losses, after markets took another battering Thursday.

In recent trading:

  • Stock futures rose modestly. On Thursday, the S&P 500 lost 1.8%, closing at its lowest level since Nov. 4, the day before President Trump's election. The Nasdaq Composite fell 2.6% to end in correction territory.
  • Chip stocks rebounded. Broadcom's upbeat results sent its stock up 12% before the bell. Other semiconductor stocks such as Nvidia and ASML also climbed.
  • U.S. Treasury yields edged down Friday, as the global bond rout eased. European bond yields were also mostly lower, after a historic selloff this week.
  • The greenback weakened further. The WSJ Dollar Index dropped to its lowest level since the days after President Trump's election in November. The euro strengthened toward $1.09.
  • The European stock rally paused. Benchmarks retreated about 1% in Germany and France. They are still on track for weekly gains, after a surge in European defense stocks.

Coming up:

  • The February jobs report is due at 8:30 a.m. ET. Nonfarm payrolls likely grew by 170,000 jobs, according to consensus estimates, up from 143,000 in January. Unemployment is seen holding steady at 4%.
  • Federal Reserve officials, including Jerome Powell, are set to make their last public remarks before the central bank's next rate decision. The Fed chair is due to discuss the economic outlook at 12:30 p.m.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: HPE Stock Tumbles on Weak Earnings Forecast. Company Cutting 2,500 Jobs. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Angela Palumbo

Hewlett Packard Enterprise reported mixed first-quarter financials and a second-quarter outlook that widely missed the mark set by Wall Street.

HPE posted adjusted first-quarter earnings of 49 cents a share on revenue of $7.85 billion. Analysts surveyed by FactSet were expecting the company to report earnings of 50 cents a share on revenue of $7.81 billion.

In the same period last year, HPE reported earnings of 48 cents a share on revenue of $6.76 billion.

The company also said it expects adjusted second-quarter earnings to be between 28 cents a share to 34 cents a share on revenue of $7.2 billion to $7.6 billion. That's below Wall Street's estimates for 50 cents a share on revenue of $7.92 billion. For the full fiscal year, the company expects adjusted earnings of $1.70 to $1.90, versus analysts' current forecast of $2.13.

Shares of HPE were down 18% in after-hours trading following the earnings release.

The company's server segment brings in the majority of revenue. HPE reported server revenue of $4.3 billion in the first quarter, which was up 29% from the same period last year and just above Wall Street estimates for $4.26 billion. However, HPE posted an 8.1% operating margin for the server segment, down from 11.4% last year.

"We could have executed better on a couple of areas, which put further pressure in the server business," CEO Antonio Neri said in an interview with Barron's.

One of the issues that impacted the server business came as the company transitions to Nvidia's Blackwell graphics processing units. Neri said "we had a higher than normal AI inventory because of the rapid transition to the next generation GPUs."

But Neri expressed confidence in what's ahead. He said he's "pleased with the market demand that we have seen. We have seen double-digit demand in servers, triple-digit demand in our storage offering, [and] double-digit demand in our networking business."

The company also announced a cost-reduction program in order to focus on profitable growth. The program is expected to be implemented through fiscal year 2026 and deliver gross savings of approximately $350 million by fiscal year 2027. Neri told Barron's the moves would result in the elimination of 2,500 jobs.

Shares of HPE have declined 15% this year. In late January, the Justice Department moved to block the planned acquisition of Juniper Networks — a networking company — by HPE. A trial regarding the acquisition begins in July.

Tech stocks more broadly have been under pressure recently as Wall Street sells shares of highly valued companies to mitigate risks in an uncertain environment, driven by tariffs.

"It is a very fluid, complicated situation, with a lot of moving parts and twists," Neri said of tariffs, adding that the company does a lot of manufacturing in Mexico, which could lead to impacts down the line. He added "we guided the best knowledge we have at this point in time. We have had a proactive process to analyze multiple scenarios since December, and this is what we know today."

Write to Angela Palumbo at angela.palumbo@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
